ch1.mainTitle: Causes of Upper Lobe Pulmonary Fibrosis

differentials.dominantImaging: Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with upper lobe fibrosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Perilymphatic nodules with symmetrical hilar and paratracheal adenopathy

differentials.dominantImaging: Apical cavitation, consolidation, and tree-in-bud nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Asymmetric apical cavities lacking significant mediastinal lymphadenopathy

differentials.dominantImaging: Paramediastinal fibrosis with sharp non-anatomical geometric borders
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Strict geometric margins conforming to previous radiation therapy portals

differentials.dominantImaging: Centrilobular nodules, ground-glass opacities, and mosaic attenuation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Headcheese pattern with lobular air trapping sparing lung bases

differentials.dominantImaging: Conglomerate upper lobe masses and eggshell nodal calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Progressive massive fibrosis migrating from lung periphery toward hila

differentials.dominantImaging: Apical fibrobullous disease and upper lobe fibrosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated with bamboo spine, syndesmophytes, and secondary mycetoma formation

differentials.dominantImaging: Upper lobe reticulonodular opacities mimicking pulmonary sarcoidosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Radiologically indistinguishable from sarcoidosis but features occupational aerospace exposure

differentials.dominantImaging: Upper lobe predominant cystic and tubular bronchiectasis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Extensive mucoid impaction and hyperinflation typically seen in youth

ch1.clinicalPearl: Always check the lower cervical and thoracic spine for syndesmophytes to catch Ankylosing Spondylitis as a subtle cause of upper lobe fibrosis.

ch01.patternSummary: Upper lobe pulmonary fibrosis has a significantly restricted differential diagnosis compared to lower lobe fibrosis, typically resulting from inhaled dusts, specific infections, or targeted radiation [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral hilar adenopathy with upper zone fibrosis [2]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: perilymphatic nodules along fissures and bronchovascular bundles [3]
supportingFeatures: Symmetrical hilar and right paratracheal adenopathy (1-2-3 sign) [4]
Egg-shell nodal calcification may be present [5]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: upper lobe consolidation with cavitation and fibrosis [6]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: centrilobular tree-in-bud nodules indicating active endobronchial spread [6]
supportingFeatures: Prefers posterior segments of the upper lobes [5]
Associated volume loss and architectural distortion [5]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: paramediastinal fibrosis with sharp geometric straight margins [7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: sharp non-anatomical borders conforming to radiation portals [7]
supportingFeatures: Strictly confined to previous radiotherapy fields [7]
Volume loss in the irradiated lung area [7]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: centrilobular nodules with ground-glass and mosaic attenuation [8]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: headcheese pattern with expiratory lobular air trapping [9]
supportingFeatures: Relative sparing of the lung bases [10]
History of exposure to organic antigens [1]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: conglomerate upper lobe masses with background nodularity [11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: progressive massive fibrosis migrating towards the hila [12]
supportingFeatures: Egg-shell calcification of hilar lymph nodes [13]
Occupational exposure to silica or coal dust [13]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: apical fibrobullous disease with upward hilar retraction [14]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: coexistent bamboo spine and bilateral symmetrical sacroiliitis [14]
supportingFeatures: Often complicated by secondary aspergilloma (mycetoma) within cavities [15]
Flowing syndesmophytes on spinal imaging [16]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: reticulonodular opacities radiologically identical to pulmonary sarcoidosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: occupational aerospace or high-tech metal exposure history
supportingFeatures: Mimics sarcoidosis radiologically and histologically
Hilar lymphadenopathy may be present

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: upper lobe predominant cystic and cylindrical bronchiectasis [17]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: extensive mucoid impaction and hyperinflation in youth [17]
supportingFeatures: Hyperinflation and marked air trapping [17]
Finger-in-glove opacities resulting from mucoid impaction [18]

ch01.clinicalPearl: Ankylosing spondylitis causes upper lobe fibrosis, making it unique among connective tissue diseases like rheumatoid arthritis and scleroderma which characteristically cause lower lobe fibrosis [14, 19].

ch02.patternSummary: Diffuse cystic interstitial lung disease typically presents with preserved or increased lung volumes, helping to differentiate it from fibrotic lung diseases which cause volume loss.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Uniform thin-walled cysts lacking zonal predominance
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Involves costophrenic angles, uniform cyst size, lacks nodules
supportingFeatures: Occurs almost exclusively in females of childbearing age
Commonly associated with chylous pleural effusions and pneumothoraces

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Bizarre irregularly shaped cysts with cavitating nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Upper lobe predominance, classically spares the costophrenic angles
supportingFeatures: Strongly associated with a history of cigarette smoking
Early phase centrilobular nodules progress to bizarre cysts

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Apical cysts and bullae with basal fibrosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Coexistent ribbon ribs and cutaneous or mediastinal neurofibromas
supportingFeatures: Dysplastic ribbon-like ribs or rib erosions
Posterior mediastinal masses (paraspinal neurofibromas)

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse uniform thin-walled cysts identical to LAM
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Identical to LAM but with renal or CNS lesions
supportingFeatures: Coexistent renal angiomyolipomas
Multisystem involvement including CNS tubers and cardiac rhabdomyomas

ch02.clinicalPearl: LAM and Tuberous Sclerosis feature uniform cysts that involve the costophrenic angles, whereas P-LCH features bizarrely shaped cysts with nodules that characteristically spare the costophrenic angles.

ch03.patternSummary: A reticulonodular pattern represents a combination of intersecting linear interstitial opacities and micronodules, requiring careful assessment of nodule distribution and lung volumes to narrow the differential.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: perilymphatic nodules with symmetric bilateral hilar adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: nodules along fissures and bronchovascular bundles, Garland's triad
supportingFeatures: Mid to upper zone predominance
Can lead to upper lobe fibrosis in end-stage disease

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: centrilobular and subpleural nodules with upper zone predominance
differentials.keyDiscriminator: eggshell nodal calcification and progressive massive fibrosis masses
supportingFeatures: History of occupational exposure (e.g., mining, sandblasting)
Nodules typically migrate from periphery towards the hila

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: irregular beaded or nodular interlobular septal thickening
differentials.keyDiscriminator: asymmetric presentation with preserved underlying secondary lobule architecture
supportingFeatures: Frequently associated with unilateral pleural effusions
Secondary to malignancies like breast, lung, or stomach carcinoma

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse uniform millet-seed-sized pulmonary nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: random uniform 1-3mm distribution indicating hematogenous dissemination
supportingFeatures: Can occur alongside apical cavitating consolidation
Nodules are well-defined but do not calcify acutely

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple variable pulmonary nodules which frequently cavitate
differentials.keyDiscriminator: waxing and waning cavitary nodules with alveolar hemorrhage
supportingFeatures: Classic for Wegener granulomatosis (granulomatosis with polyangiitis)
Often presents clinically with hemoptysis and renal impairment

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: centrilobular nodules progressing into bizarre thin-walled cysts
differentials.keyDiscriminator: upper lobe predominance strictly sparing the costophrenic angles
supportingFeatures: Very strongly associated with a history of cigarette smoking
Preserved or increased overall lung volumes

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: subpleural reticulation and basal honeycombing with volume loss
differentials.keyDiscriminator: lower zone peripheral predominance with traction bronchiectasis
supportingFeatures: The hallmark imaging pattern of usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP)
Lack of significant ground-glass opacification or nodules

ch03.clinicalPearl: Always classify the sub-pattern of the nodules to narrow this list: perilymphatic distribution favors sarcoidosis or lymphangitis, centrilobular favors histiocytosis, and a random distribution indicates miliary TB or metastases.

ch04.patternSummary: Multiple pulmonary nodules have a broad differential that is best narrowed by assessing nodule size variation, distribution, and the presence of cavitation or calcification.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Randomly distributed nodules of varying sizes
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Disparate sizes with basal predilection and no calcification
supportingFeatures: Look for surgical clips, missing breast shadow, or destructive bone lesions
Squamous cell and sarcoma metastases frequently cavitate

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Multiple thick-walled cavitating pulmonary nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Waxing and waning cavitations often with alveolar hemorrhage
supportingFeatures: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) classically lacks calcification
Rheumatoid nodules have a peripheral and lower zone predilection

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Peripheral cavitating nodules with feeding vessel sign
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Wedge-shaped or cavitary lesions with prominent feeding vessels
supportingFeatures: Frequently multiloculated and rapidly changing in appearance
Strong clinical association with intravenous drug abuse or endocarditis

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Upper lobe cavitating nodules with tree-in-bud opacities
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Centrilobular tree-in-bud satellites indicating active endobronchial spread
supportingFeatures: Fungal nodules (e.g., aspergillosis) may show a surrounding ground-glass halo
Tuberculous nodules are often accompanied by volume loss and fibrosis

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Peripheral lucencies evolving within focal pulmonary contusions
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Post-traumatic pneumatoceles forming within areas of lung contusion
supportingFeatures: Associated with rib fractures, pneumothorax, or subcutaneous emphysema
Follows a clear history of blunt thoracic trauma

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Upper lobe nodules with progressive massive fibrosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Eggshell nodal calcification with nodules migrating towards hila
supportingFeatures: Nodules coalesce into large conglomerate upper lobe masses
Clear history of occupational exposure to silica or coal dust

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Peripheral ground-glass opacities and nodular consolidation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Photographic negative of pulmonary edema with extreme peripheral predilection
supportingFeatures: Classically affects young adult females with an asthma history
Prompt response to corticosteroid therapy

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Perilymphatic micronodules with bilateral hilar adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Nodules distributed strictly along fissures and central bronchovascular bundles
supportingFeatures: Garland's triad: bilateral hilar and right paratracheal adenopathy
Mid and upper zone predominance with later fibrotic distortion

ch04.clinicalPearl: Metastatic disease is the most common cause of multiple pulmonary nodules; always scrutinize the film for destructive bone lesions, surgical clips, or an absent breast shadow.

ch05.patternSummary: Miliary shadowing consists of countless discrete 1-2mm pulmonary opacities that require careful assessment of distribution and size uniformity to narrow the differential.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse uniform randomly distributed 1-2mm pulmonary nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: uniform size and random distribution, lack of acute calcification
supportingFeatures: Represents haematogenous dissemination
May have associated apical cavitation or consolidation

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: randomly distributed pulmonary nodules of varying sizes
differentials.keyDiscriminator: disparate nodule sizes with a lower lobe predilection
supportingFeatures: Classically from thyroid, renal, breast, or melanoma primaries
Look for surgical clips, missing breast shadows, or bone lesions

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: perilymphatic micronodules with symmetric bilateral hilar adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: nodules distributed strictly along fissures and bronchovascular bundles
supportingFeatures: Garland's triad: bilateral hilar and right paratracheal adenopathy
Mid and upper zone predominance

ch05.clinicalPearl: When evaluating a miliary pattern, remember that miliary metastases are classically thyroid or melanoma in origin, while miliary TB nodules uniquely do not calcify acutely.

ch06.patternSummary: The presence of multiple calcified pulmonary nodules significantly narrows the differential diagnosis, heavily relying on nodule distribution, size variation, and associated cardiovascular or osseous findings.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Randomly distributed calcified nodules of varying sizes
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Disparate sizes with a lower lobe predilection
supportingFeatures: Classically from osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma, papillary thyroid, or mucinous carcinomas
Look for missing limbs, surgical clips, or destructive bone lesions

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Well-defined solitary nodule with popcorn calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Focal deposits of macroscopic fat and popcorn calcifications
supportingFeatures: Most common benign tumor of the lung
Typically circumscribed and less than 4 cm in diameter

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Multiple irregular nodules with amorphous calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Varying sized nodules calcifying in up to 50%
supportingFeatures: May be associated with diffuse septal thickening
Can also present with tracheobronchial wall calcification

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Perilymphatic calcified micronodules with bilateral hilar adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Egg-shell nodal calcification with upper zone nodularity
supportingFeatures: Nodules distributed along fissures and bronchovascular bundles
Often evolves into upper lobe fibrosis and architectural distortion

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Small diffuse calcified nodules in mid-to-lower zones
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated with cardiomegaly and left atrial enlargement
supportingFeatures: Represents pulmonary ossification secondary to chronic venous hypertension
Look for a double right heart border and splayed carina

ch06.clinicalPearl: A classic 'Aunt Minnie' case for calcified metastases is multiple calcified lung nodules of varying sizes associated with a sclerotic or absent proximal humerus, diagnostic of osteosarcoma.

ch07.patternSummary: Evaluating a large cavitating lung lesion depends heavily on wall thickness, inner margin irregularity, and the presence of air-fluid levels or satellite nodules.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Solitary mass with thick, irregular inner walls
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Irregular thick wall, classic for squamous cell carcinoma
supportingFeatures: Squamous cell carcinomas and sarcomas frequently cavitate.
Primary tumors are typically solitary and may invade ribs.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Thick-walled cavity containing an air-fluid level
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Continuous regular smooth 2-7mm ring
supportingFeatures: Strongly associated with lower lobe aspiration.
Staphylococcus and Klebsiella are common pyogenic pathogens.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Apical cavitating consolidation or multiple random nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Tree-in-bud satellites (TB) or multiple waxing cavities (GPA)
supportingFeatures: TB favors upper lobe posterior segments.
Wegener's (GPA) lacks calcification and may hemorrhage.

ch07.clinicalPearl: Squamous cell carcinomas commonly cavitate with thick, irregular walls, whereas a smooth, regular 2-7mm ring with an air-fluid level is highly characteristic of a pyogenic abscess.

ch08.patternSummary: Anterior mediastinal masses typically efface the heart borders and ascending aorta, forming an obtuse angle with the chest wall [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Solid anterior mass invading mediastinal fat or pleura
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Pleural drop metastases and local mediastinal fat invasion
supportingFeatures: Typically affects older adults [1]
Associated with myasthenia gravis [3]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Well-defined mass containing macroscopic fat and calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Presence of fat, enhancing septations, and calcified teeth/bone
supportingFeatures: Predominantly presents in younger patients during their second to fourth decades [1, 4]
Mostly benign, but may contain solid or cystic components [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Bulky lobulated mass with multi-compartment lymphadenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Concomitant middle/posterior mediastinal or distant lymph node enlargement
supportingFeatures: Hodgkin disease almost always involves the superior mediastinal nodes [5]
Associated splenomegaly is a common finding [6]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Superior mediastinal mass contiguous with the cervical gland
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Cervicothoracic contiguity causing focal deviation of the trachea
supportingFeatures: Avidly enhances with intravenous contrast [7]
Cervicothoracic sign: the well-defined margin fades out above the clavicles [8]

ch08.clinicalPearl: Patient age and compartmental spread are key discriminators: germ cell tumors favor younger patients, thymomas favor older adults, and lymphoma almost always involves lymph nodes outside the anterior compartment [1].

ch09.patternSummary: Pleural lesions typically form an obtuse angle with the chest wall, and differentiating benign from malignant causes relies heavily on assessing pleural thickness, nodularity, and mediastinal involvement [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Circumferential nodular pleural thickening with volume loss [1, 3].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Involves mediastinal pleura, often greater than 1cm thick [2, 3].
supportingFeatures: Associated with calcified pleural plaques from asbestos exposure [1].
Causes ipsilateral volume loss and restricted mediastinal shift [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Lentiform pleural fluid collection with enhancing walls [2].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Split pleura sign with visceral and parietal enhancement [2].
supportingFeatures: Forms an obtuse angle, convex towards the lung [1].
Often associated with adjacent consolidation or pneumonia [2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Unilateral pleural effusion with nodular pleural thickening [4].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Evolves into severe fibrothorax with dense sheet-like calcification [4].
supportingFeatures: Associated with apical cavitation or tree-in-bud nodules [5].
A common cause of massive fibrothorax late in disease [4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Bilateral calcified parietal and diaphragmatic pleural plaques [6, 7].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Discontinuous plateau-like plaques that typically spare the apices [8].
supportingFeatures: Strongly associated with rounded atelectasis (comet tail sign) [9].
Latent period of 20 to 30 years after exposure [10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Large unilateral pleural effusion with nodular pleural deposits [1, 2].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Presence of a primary lung mass or rib destruction [2, 11].
supportingFeatures: Most common cause of malignant pleural effusion [12].
Adenocarcinoma is the most frequent histological subtype [13].

ch09.clinicalPearl: Malignant pleural thickening, such as mesothelioma, characteristically involves the mediastinal pleura and is circumferentially >1cm thick, whereas benign asbestos plaques classically spare the mediastinal pleura and lung apices [2, 3].

ch10.patternSummary: A hyperlucent hemithorax is caused by a reduction in soft tissue, decreased pulmonary blood flow, or increased regional air, requiring systematic anatomical evaluation [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Visible visceral pleural edge lacking peripheral markings
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Absence of lung markings distal to pleural line
supportingFeatures: Deep sulcus sign on supine radiographs [3]
Look for contralateral mediastinal shift indicating tension [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Unilateral small hyperlucent lung with reduced vasculature
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Reduced lung volume with expiratory air trapping
supportingFeatures: Acquired post-infective bronchiolitis in childhood [1]
Small ipsilateral hilum and pulmonary artery [5]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Hyperlucent expanded lung with contralateral mediastinal shift
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Contralateral mediastinal shift exacerbated on expiratory views
supportingFeatures: Often due to inhaled foreign body (e.g., peanut) in children [5]
Congenital lobar emphysema presents similarly in neonates [6]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Focal avascular radiolucency with thin curvilinear walls
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Hairline curvilinear wall distinct from visceral pleural line
supportingFeatures: May compress adjacent normal lung parenchyma [7]
Apical predilection in centrilobular emphysema [8]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Unilateral hyperlucency with normal underlying pulmonary vasculature
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Absent breast shadow or axillary fold
supportingFeatures: Look for surgical clips in the ipsilateral axilla [6]
Poland syndrome is a congenital alternative lacking pectoralis muscle [9, 10]

ch10.clinicalPearl: Always evaluate the pulmonary vasculature first; normal vessels in a hyperlucent lung suggest an extrapulmonary cause like mastectomy, whereas reduced vessels point to a pulmonary or airway etiology [1].

ch11.patternSummary: A completely opacified hemithorax requires immediate assessment of tracheal and mediastinal position to determine if the underlying process is volume-losing or volume-expanding [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Complete opacification with ipsilateral mediastinal shift [1]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Tracheal deviation and shift towards the opacification [1]
supportingFeatures: Check endotracheal tube position for mainstem intubation [1]
Look for a central obstructing tumor or metastasis [1]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Complete opacification with contralateral mediastinal shift [1]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Tracheal deviation and shift away from the opacification [1]
supportingFeatures: Commonly malignant if large; look for contralateral nodules [1]
Associated with lung/breast carcinoma or lymphoma [2]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Unilateral opacification with absent pulmonary structures [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Complete absence of pulmonary artery and main bronchus
supportingFeatures: Tracheal deviation to the ipsilateral side [3]
No change compared with previous chest radiographs [3]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Circumferential nodular pleural thickening with volume loss [4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Limited mediastinal shift due to malignant lung encasement [2, 3]
supportingFeatures: Presence of calcified pleural plaques indicating asbestos exposure [3, 4]
May involve the mediastinal pleura and interlobar fissures [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Completely opacified hemithorax with ipsilateral volume loss [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Resected fifth or sixth rib and surgical clips [3]
supportingFeatures: Tracheal deviation and mediastinal shift to the ipsilateral side [3]
History or clear signs of previous thoracotomy [3]

ch11.clinicalPearl: The position of the trachea is the single most critical discriminator; it shifts towards a collapse or pneumonectomy, but away from a large pleural effusion [2].

ch12.patternSummary: Peripheral consolidation represents air-space filling that classically spares the central perihilar regions, requiring assessment of symptom chronicity, eosinophil counts, and pulmonary vasculature to narrow the diagnosis.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Patchy peripheral consolidation with ground-glass halos
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Reversed halo (atoll) sign with lower zone predilection
supportingFeatures: Typically affects patients aged 40–70 years
Fails to respond to antibiotics but rapidly improves with steroids

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Upper lobe predominant peripheral consolidation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Photographic negative of pulmonary edema
supportingFeatures: Classically affects young adult females with an asthma history
Associated with peripheral blood eosinophilia

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Wedge-shaped peripheral consolidation along the pleura
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Hampton hump with ipsilateral pulmonary artery filling defect
supportingFeatures: Typically multi-focal with a lower lobe predominance
Frequently associated with ipsilateral pleural effusions

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Peripheral consolidation with underlying subpleural nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Coexistent bilateral hilar and right paratracheal lymphadenopathy
supportingFeatures: Mid and upper zone disease distribution
Nodularity along the fissures and bronchovascular bundles

ch12.clinicalPearl: While COP and chronic eosinophilic pneumonia can look identical, eosinophilic pneumonia classically favors the upper lobes, whereas COP typically involves the lower lobes.

ch13.patternSummary: Diffuse bilateral consolidation represents air-space filling that demands rapid differentiation between cardiogenic edema, hemorrhage, opportunistic infections, and specific interstitial processes.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Central bat-wing consolidation with cardiomegaly
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated cardiomegaly, Kerley B lines, and pleural effusions
supportingFeatures: Cephalization of pulmonary vessels
Rapidly shifting opacities reflecting fluid changes

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Crazy-paving pattern with ground-glass opacities
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Geographic distribution sparing apices and costophrenic sulci
supportingFeatures: Subpleural sparing is seen in up to 50% of cases
Often requires bronchoalveolar lavage for definitive diagnosis

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Perihilar ground-glass opacities and consolidation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Development of upper lobe pneumatoceles leading to pneumothorax
supportingFeatures: Strongly associated with immunocompromised states
Pleural effusions and lymphadenopathy are notably rare

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Multifocal consolidation with cavitating nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Waxing and waning cavitations lacking calcification
supportingFeatures: May present with concurrent alveolar hemorrhage
Clinical triad includes sinus, lung, and renal disease

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse ground-glass opacities and confluent consolidation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Rapidly clearing or changing opacities over hours
supportingFeatures: Clinical presentation of hemoptysis and drop in hemoglobin
Underlying causes include Goodpasture syndrome and SLE

ch13.clinicalPearl: In an immunocompromised patient, diffuse bilateral consolidation without effusions is PJP until proven otherwise, whereas the presence of cardiomegaly and effusions virtually guarantees heart failure.

ch14.patternSummary: Bilateral hilar enlargement requires distinguishing between vascular and nodal causes; lobulated contours suggest lymphadenopathy, whereas smooth margins with peripheral pruning suggest pulmonary artery enlargement.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: symmetrical lobulated bilateral hilar and paratracheal adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Garland's triad (1-2-3 sign) with perilymphatic nodules
supportingFeatures: Most common cause of bilateral symmetric hilar enlargement
Often associated with mid and upper zone reticulonodular opacities

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral hilar adenopathy with eggshell nodal calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: eggshell calcification with upper zone progressive massive fibrosis
supportingFeatures: Requires long-term occupational exposure to free silica
Nodules tend to migrate from the periphery towards the hila

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: asymmetrical bulky bilateral hilar and mediastinal adenopathy
differentials.keyDiscriminator: asymmetrical involvement typically abutting the cardiac borders
supportingFeatures: Anterior and superior mediastinal nodes are frequently involved
Hodgkin disease almost always involves superior mediastinal nodes

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bulky symmetric hilar and mediastinal lymph node enlargement
differentials.keyDiscriminator: symmetric bulky nodes with massive splenomegaly
supportingFeatures: Typically seen in chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL)
May present with striking supraclavicular and axillary adenopathy

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral hilar adenopathy with patchy airspace consolidation
differentials.keyDiscriminator: unilateral in 95% of TB; viral in children
supportingFeatures: Primary TB is rarely bilateral and symmetrical
Viral etiologies are the most common cause in children

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: hilar adenopathy with discrete upper zone background nodules
differentials.keyDiscriminator: eggshell calcification with coal or dust exposure history
supportingFeatures: Coal worker's pneumoconiosis rarely has eggshell calcification (only 1%)
Background of small diffuse nodules in mid-to-upper zones

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bulky asymmetric hilar nodes with primary lung mass
differentials.keyDiscriminator: disparate asymmetrical growth tracking from a primary tumor
supportingFeatures: Common with bronchogenic, breast, and renal cell carcinomas
Often associated with unilateral pleural effusions or lung nodules

ch14.clinicalPearl: Sarcoidosis classically presents with symmetrical, lobulated bilateral hilar and right paratracheal lymphadenopathy (Garland's triad), whereas lymphoma typically causes asymmetrical, bulky anterior mediastinal node enlargement.

ch15.patternSummary: Inferior rib notching results from the enlargement of intercostal arteries, veins, or nerves within the costal groove, requiring careful assessment of laterality and surgical history.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral notching of 3rd to 8th ribs
differentials.keyDiscriminator: figure-3 sign of the aortic arch
supportingFeatures: Classically spares the 1st and 2nd ribs
Associated with bicuspid aortic valves and Turner syndrome

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral notching of upper 3 to 4 ribs
differentials.keyDiscriminator: ipsilateral thoracotomy or sternotomy wires
supportingFeatures: Palliative surgical shunt for Tetralogy of Fallot
Notching occurs exclusively on the side of the procedure

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: thin attenuated irregular ribbon-like ribs
differentials.keyDiscriminator: coexistent posterior mediastinal masses and kyphoscoliosis
supportingFeatures: Notching reflects both primary bone dysplasia and pressure from neurofibromas
May also present with cutaneous nodules and interstitial lung disease

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral inferior rib notching without surgical hardware
differentials.keyDiscriminator: asymmetric involvement classically affecting the left side
supportingFeatures: Secondary to collateral flow bypassing the subclavian occlusion
May also occur with an aberrant subclavian artery origin

ch15.clinicalPearl: In aortic coarctation, bilateral rib notching classically spares the 1st and 2nd ribs because their arterial supply (the supreme intercostal artery) arises from the costocervical trunk proximal to the stenosis.

ch16.patternSummary: Diffuse osteosclerosis represents a generalized increase in bone density that requires careful evaluation of vertebral body patterns, bone morphology, and extra-skeletal findings to narrow the differential.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Rugger-jersey spine with ill-defined endplate sclerosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Subperiosteal bone resorption and smudgy sclerotic endplate margins
supportingFeatures: Vascular and soft tissue calcifications
Presence of hemodialysis lines or a renal transplant

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse dense bones with sharp sandwich vertebrae
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Sharp sclerotic endplate margins and bone-in-bone appearance
supportingFeatures: Erlenmeyer flask deformity of the long bones
Pediatric onset with brittle bones and frequent fractures

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Uniform diffuse osteosclerosis with massive splenomegaly
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Massive splenomegaly with uniformly very low T1/T2 marrow signal
supportingFeatures: Typically presents in patients older than 50 years
Extramedullary hematopoiesis leading to hepatosplenomegaly

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Bony enlargement with picture frame vertebral bodies
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Bone enlargement, coarsened trabeculae, and cortical thickening
supportingFeatures: Thickening of the iliopectineal and ilioischial lines in the pelvis
Cotton wool appearance of the skull in later stages

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse osteosclerosis with H-shaped biconcave vertebrae
differentials.keyDiscriminator: H-shaped central endplate depressions with a calcified spleen
supportingFeatures: Avascular necrosis of the femoral or humeral heads
Increased incidence of radiopaque gallstones

ch16.clinicalPearl: The 'rugger jersey' spine of renal osteodystrophy features smudgy, ill-defined margins, whereas the 'sandwich' vertebrae seen in osteopetrosis have sharp, distinct interfaces between the sclerotic endplates and lucent central bone.

ch17.patternSummary: FOG MACHINES (or FEGNOMASHIC) outlines the classic differential for benign and malignant lytic bone lesions, best narrowed by patient age, lesion margin, and specific anatomical location [1-4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: ground-glass matrix with a thick sclerotic rim [5, 6]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: ground-glass appearance with thick rind of reactive bone [5, 6]
supportingFeatures: Shepherd's crook deformity of the proximal femur [5, 7]
No periosteal reaction unless complicated by fracture [8, 9]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lytic lesion with bone sequestrum and involucrum [10-12]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: dense sequestrum surrounded by radiolucent area and involucrum [10, 11]
supportingFeatures: Often accompanied by periosteal reaction and soft tissue gas [12, 13]
May form a cloaca or draining sinus tract [10, 11]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric expansile lytic lesion abutting articular surface [14, 15]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: closed epiphyses, eccentric location, lacks a sclerotic margin [14, 16, 17]
supportingFeatures: Most commonly occurs around the knee and distal radius [18, 19]
Demonstrates a narrow zone of transition but no sclerotic rim [17]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple punched-out lytic lesions without sclerotic margins [20-22]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: occurs in older adults, often destroys vertebral pedicles [23, 24]
supportingFeatures: Endosteal scalloping is a common finding [21, 22]
Often presents with diffuse osteopenia or vertebral compression fractures [25, 26]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: expansile blow-out lesion with internal fluid-fluid levels [27, 28]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: multiple fluid-fluid levels on MRI resulting from hemorrhage [27, 29, 30]
supportingFeatures: Commonly occurs in metaphyses or posterior spinal elements [31, 32]
Typically affects patients younger than 30 years old [29, 33]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined epiphyseal lucency with thin sclerotic rim [34, 35]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: epiphyseal location in patients with open growth plates [16, 35, 36]
supportingFeatures: Internal calcifications are seen in approximately 50% of cases [34, 35]
Associated with significant surrounding marrow and soft tissue edema [35, 37]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined lytic lesion with subperiosteal bone resorption [5, 38]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subperiosteal resorption along radial aspect of middle phalanges [38, 39]
supportingFeatures: Associated with diffuse osteosclerosis, such as a rugger jersey spine [5, 38]
Dystrophic soft tissue calcifications may be present [5, 38]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: metaphyseal lucency surrounded by thick reactive sclerosis [40, 41]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: penumbra sign on T1 MRI with peripheral enhancement [42, 43]
supportingFeatures: Lucent tortuous channel extending toward the growth plate [40, 41]
Double line sign on T2-weighted MRI sequences [40, 44]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric lobulated metaphyseal lucency with sclerotic margin [45]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: asymptomatic, resolves with age, completely lacks periosteal reaction [45, 46]
supportingFeatures: Migrates toward the center of the diaphysis as bone grows [45]
The scalloped sclerotic margin is typically V- or U-shaped [45]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: central medullary lesion with rings and arcs calcification [47, 48]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: chondroid matrix calcification without periosteal reaction or pain [47, 49]
supportingFeatures: Most common in the small tubular bones of the hands and feet [50, 51]
Multiple lesions are seen in Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome [52, 53]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: centrally located metaphyseal lucent bone cyst [54, 55]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: fallen fragment sign reliably indicating a pathologic fracture [54, 56]
supportingFeatures: Most commonly occurs in the proximal humerus and proximal femur [54, 57]
Strictly spares the epiphysis and stays centrally placed [55, 58]

ch17.clinicalPearl: Patient age is the most crucial differentiator; lesions like chondroblastoma, aneurysmal bone cysts, and simple bone cysts occur almost exclusively in patients under 30 with open physes, while giant cell tumors almost exclusively occur after physeal closure [4, 14, 16, 34].

ch18.patternSummary: The FEGNOMASHIC mnemonic outlines the classic differential for expansile lytic bone lesions, which is most effectively narrowed by assessing the patient's age, epiphyseal closure, and specific lesion margins [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: ground-glass matrix with thick sclerotic rind
differentials.keyDiscriminator: ground-glass appearance with a thick reactive rind
supportingFeatures: Shepherd's crook deformity of the proximal femur [4, 5].
Lacks a periosteal reaction unless complicated by fracture [5, 6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lytic lesion with beveled edges or button sequestrum
differentials.keyDiscriminator: beveled edges in skull or isolated vertebra plana
supportingFeatures: Occurs predominantly in patients under 30 years old [7].
May present as a solitary vertebra plana maintaining disc spaces [8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric subarticular expansile lesion without sclerotic margin
differentials.keyDiscriminator: abuts articular surface in patients with closed physes
supportingFeatures: Typically occurs around the knee or distal radius [9, 10].
Narrow zone of transition strictly lacking a sclerotic border [10, 11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric lobulated metaphyseal lucency with scalloped sclerotic border
differentials.keyDiscriminator: entirely asymptomatic without periostitis in patients under 30
supportingFeatures: Migrates toward the center of the diaphysis and resolves with age [12].
Most commonly seen about the knee [13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: expansile lesion larger than 2cm in posterior spine
differentials.keyDiscriminator: expansile posterior element spine mass mimicking ABC
supportingFeatures: May demonstrate speckled internal matrix calcifications [14].
Often surrounded by peritumoral edema extending beyond the lesion [15].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple punched-out lytic lesions with destructive permeative appearance
differentials.keyDiscriminator: occurs exclusively in patients over 40 years old
supportingFeatures: Renal and thyroid metastases characteristically produce highly expansile lesions [16].
Plasmacytoma may demonstrate a unique mini-brain appearance on MRI [17].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: expansile blow-out lesion with internal fluid-fluid levels
differentials.keyDiscriminator: multiple fluid-fluid levels representing blood breakdown products
supportingFeatures: Typically affects patients younger than 30 years old [18].
Doughnut sign on bone scintigraphy showing peripheral uptake with photopenic center [19, 20].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: central metaphyseal lucency with fallen fragment sign
differentials.keyDiscriminator: fallen fragment sign indicating a pathologic fracture
supportingFeatures: Most commonly occurs in the proximal humerus and femur [21].
Centrally located within the medullary cavity [22].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined lytic lesion with subperiosteal bone resorption
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subperiosteal resorption classically along radial middle phalanges
supportingFeatures: Associated with generalized osteopenia or osteosclerosis (rugger jersey spine) [4, 23].
Dystrophic soft-tissue calcifications may be present [18].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lytic metaphyseal lesion with lucent tortuous channel
differentials.keyDiscriminator: lucent tortuous channel extending toward the growth plate
supportingFeatures: May demonstrate a dense bony sequestrum [24].
Penumbra sign on T1-weighted MRI sequences [25, 26].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric expansile metaphyseal lucency with scalloped sclerotic border
differentials.keyDiscriminator: resembles nonossifying fibroma but commonly presents with pain
supportingFeatures: Oriented along the long axis of the host bone [27].
May contain coarse, thick internal trabeculae [28].

ch18.clinicalPearl: Patient age is the ultimate discriminator for lytic lesions; giant cell tumors occur essentially only after physeal closure, while entities like aneurysmal bone cysts, nonossifying fibromas, and chondroblastomas present almost exclusively in patients under 30 [2, 3, 13].

ch19.patternSummary: Aggressive lytic bone lesions in children are characterized by a wide zone of transition and cortical destruction, necessitating prompt differentiation between infection, sarcomas, and metastases [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Transverse lucent metaphyseal bands and diffuse osteopenia [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Bilateral symmetric metaphyseal lucency in young children [4]
supportingFeatures: Periosteal new bone formation secondary to subperiosteal infiltration [5]
Peak incidence occurs between 2 and 5 years of age [5]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Ill-defined lytic lesion with sequestrum and periostitis [6, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Infection can cross the open physis in infants [8, 9]
supportingFeatures: Thick irregular reactive sclerosis in subacute or chronic phases [7]
Often affects the metaphysis due to slow-flowing venous sinusoids [8]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Permeative diaphyseal lucency with onion-skin periostitis [10]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Permeative diaphyseal lesion with disproportionate soft-tissue mass [11, 12]
supportingFeatures: Peak incidence between 10 and 15 years of age [13]
Can present with fever and leukocytosis mimicking infection [14]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Punched-out lytic skull lesions with beveled edges [15, 16]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Beveled edges, button sequestrum, or vertebra plana [15, 16]
supportingFeatures: Predominantly affects children and young adults under 30 [17]
Unlike many tumors, it can cross an open growth plate [18]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Permeative lytic lesions with primary adrenal mass [19, 20]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Age under five with a calcified suprarenal mass [19]
supportingFeatures: Most common pediatric malignancy to metastasize to bone [20, 21]
Frequently involves the skull base and orbits [22]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Metaphyseal lytic-sclerotic mass with sunburst periostitis [1, 23]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Cloud-like osteoid matrix mineralization in the soft tissues [24]
supportingFeatures: Most commonly affects the distal femur or proximal tibia [24]
Associated with Codman triangle periosteal reaction [24]

ch19.clinicalPearl: The anatomical location within the long bone is highly specific; Ewing sarcoma favors the diaphysis with an onion-skin periostitis, whereas osteosarcoma typically involves the metaphysis and produces a cloud-like osteoid matrix [14, 24].

ch20.patternSummary: Epiphyseal lucent lesions represent a focused differential diagnosis strongly dictated by whether the patient's skeletal growth plates are open or closed [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric subarticular lytic lesion without sclerotic margin [4, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: abuts articular surface in patients with closed physes [3, 4]
supportingFeatures: Occurs in patients 20 to 40 years old [2, 6].
Narrow zone of transition without internal matrix mineralization [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: expansile lytic lesion larger than two centimeters [8, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: predilection for the posterior elements of the spine [10, 11]
supportingFeatures: Peritumoral edema extending beyond the lesion margin [9].
May simulate an aneurysmal bone cyst [11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined epiphyseal lucency with a sclerotic rim [12, 13]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: epiphyseal location in patients with open growth plates [14, 15]
supportingFeatures: Extensive surrounding bone marrow and soft tissue edema [16, 17].
Internal calcifications are seen in 50 percent of cases [12, 14].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: expansile epiphyseal lytic lesion with possible calcification [18, 19]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: resembles chondroblastoma but occurs after epiphyseal fusion [19]
supportingFeatures: Predominantly affects the proximal femur or proximal humerus [18, 19].
A slow-growing low-grade malignancy [19].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lytic lesion surrounded by thick reactive sclerosis [20, 21]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: lucent tortuous channel or MRI penumbra sign [6, 20, 22]
supportingFeatures: Metaphyseal origin but can cross the open physis [23].
Double line effect on T2 weighted MRI [24, 25].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined subchondral lucency with a sclerotic margin [26, 27]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subarticular location strictly without associated degenerative joint disease [27]
supportingFeatures: Multilocular cysts containing gelatinous mucoid substance [26, 27].
No evidence of communication with the joint space [26, 27].

ch20.clinicalPearl: The status of the physis is the ultimate discriminator: chondroblastomas occur before skeletal maturity, whereas giant cell tumors and clear cell chondrosarcomas occur after physeal closure [1, 3, 19].

ch21.patternSummary: Diffuse marrow infiltration on MRI replaces normal fatty marrow, characteristically demonstrating T1-weighted signal equal to or lower than adjacent intervertebral discs or muscle [1-4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse low T1 signal with variegated pattern [1, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Relative sparing of the vertebral pedicles [6]
supportingFeatures: Often presents with 'salt and pepper' or micronodular appearance [5].
May exhibit a 'mini-brain' appearance in solitary lesions [7, 8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Multifocal or diffuse low T1 marrow signal [9, 10]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Frequent destruction and involvement of vertebral pedicles [10, 11]
supportingFeatures: Often associated with an epidural soft tissue mass [12, 13].
Sclerotic metastases appear low signal on T1 and T2 [9, 10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Diffuse low T1 and T2 marrow signal [14, 15]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated thickened small bowel folds and nodules [16]
supportingFeatures: Presents with diffuse osteosclerosis on plain radiographs [15].
Associated with hepatosplenomegaly and urticaria pigmentosa [15, 17].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Uniformly very low T1 and T2 marrow signal [14, 18, 19]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Massive splenomegaly from active extramedullary hematopoiesis [18, 20, 21]
supportingFeatures: Results in diffuse osteosclerosis and 'jail-bar' ribs [22].
Typically occurs in patients over 50 years of age [20, 23].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Symmetric T1 marrow signal brighter than adjacent muscle [3, 24]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: T1 signal remains higher than muscle or adjacent disc [3, 25]
supportingFeatures: Seen in severe anemia, heavy smoking, or obesity [26, 27].
Characteristically spares the epiphyses, retaining high-signal fatty marrow [25].

ch21.clinicalPearl: Normal red marrow is always slightly higher in signal intensity than normal muscle or intervertebral discs on T1-weighted images; marrow that is isointense or hypointense to adjacent muscle on T1 is considered abnormal [3, 4].

ch22.patternSummary: Posterior vertebral body scalloping generally results from increased intraspinal pressure, dural ectasia, or congenital bone softening dysplasias [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Enlarged vertebrae or shortened pedicles causing spinal stenosis [2, 3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Heel pad >25mm or progressive caudal interpedicular narrowing [3, 4]
supportingFeatures: Enlarged paranasal sinuses and prognathism in acromegaly [3].
Champagne glass pelvic inlet with horizontal sacrum in achondroplasia [5].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Lumbosacral dural ectasia with tall elongated vertebral bodies [6, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Arachnodactyly with lumbosacral dural ectasia and severe scoliosis [7, 8]
supportingFeatures: Frequently associated with aortic root dilatation and dissection [6].
Steinberg sign demonstrating a protruding thumb from a clenched fist [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Dural ectasia causing exaggerated posterior vertebral concavity [1, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Mesenchymal tissue laxity without classic Marfan skeletal dysmorphism [1]
supportingFeatures: Patients are prone to arterial aneurysms and vascular ruptures [9].
Presents with extreme joint hypermobility and skin fragility.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Posterior scalloping with widened intervertebral neural foramina [10]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Dumbbell neurofibromas expanding the intervertebral neural foramina [11]
supportingFeatures: Sharply angled short segment scoliosis with severe kyphosis [12].
Ribbon-like deformed ribs and sphenoid wing dysplasia [13, 14].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Posterior scalloping with severe platyspondyly and anterior beaking [15, 16]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Central anterior vertebral beaking with universal vertebra plana [17, 18]
supportingFeatures: Hypoplastic dens leading to atlanto-axial instability [18].
Proximal tapering of metacarpals producing a bullet shape [19].

ch22.clinicalPearl: Always evaluate the interpedicular distance on an AP lumbar radiograph; progressive narrowing caudally strongly points to achondroplasia, while focal widening suggests an intraspinal mass or dural ectasia [4, 20].

ch23.patternSummary: Resorption of the lateral clavicle can be unilateral or bilateral, with bilateral involvement strongly suggesting systemic connective tissue or metabolic diseases [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral tapered distal clavicles with acro-osteolysis [2]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated subcutaneous and periarticular soft tissue calcification [2]
supportingFeatures: Lower lobe pulmonary fibrosis [3]
Dilated esophagus on chest radiograph [3]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral resorption with diffuse subperiosteal bone resorption [4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subperiosteal resorption on radial margins of middle phalanges [4]
supportingFeatures: Brown tumors presenting as expansile lytic lesions [4]
Osteosclerosis or 'rugger jersey' spine [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral focal distal clavicle bone loss [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strictly unilateral process without systemic joint changes [3]
supportingFeatures: May show aggressive irregular margins if osteomyelitis [5]
Post-surgical resection history if iatrogenic

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: clavicular resorption with glenohumeral joint space narrowing [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: glenohumeral erosions and loss of subacromial space [3]
supportingFeatures: May have signs of avascular necrosis from steroids [3]
Lower lobe fibrosis may be present [3]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral focal osteolysis at the acromioclavicular joint [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strictly unilateral findings with prior old fracture [3]
supportingFeatures: History of previous trauma or fracture [3]
Lacks signs of systemic inflammatory or metabolic disease [1]

ch23.clinicalPearl: Assess if the resorption is unilateral or bilateral; infection and post-traumatic osteolysis are likely unilateral, while connective tissue diseases and hyperparathyroidism are bilateral [1].

ch24.patternSummary: Short metacarpals frequently involve the fourth digit and require assessment for bilaterality, carpal deformities, and systemic calcifications to narrow the differential [1], [2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Shortening of the fourth metacarpal [3], [4].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated Madelung deformity of the wrist [3], [5].
supportingFeatures: Exclusively affects females (45XO chromosome pattern) [3], [4].
Associated with cubitus valgus and short stature [3], [4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Isolated short metacarpal without systemic changes [6], [1].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Absence of other bone or soft tissue abnormalities [6], [1].
supportingFeatures: Most common cause of a short fourth metacarpal [1].
May show evidence of prior childhood trauma or fracture [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Shortened fourth and fifth metacarpals bilaterally [3], [7].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Associated intracranial or subcutaneous soft tissue calcifications [3], [8].
supportingFeatures: Pseudopseudohypoparathyroidism shares identical imaging features but with normal calcium [9], [3].
Frequently involves the third to fifth metatarsals simultaneously [7], [2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Short metacarpal with sclerotic bone infarcts [6], [10].
differentials.keyDiscriminator: Concurrent H-shaped vertebrae or splenic calcification [11], [12].
supportingFeatures: Consequence of childhood sickle cell dactylitis causing premature physeal fusion [13], [14].
May show patchy sclerosis and residual cortical deformity [10].

ch24.clinicalPearl: Always look for a Madelung deformity at the wrist, which strongly suggests Turner syndrome in a female patient presenting with a short fourth metacarpal [3], [2].

ch25.patternSummary: Acro-osteolysis refers to the resorption or destruction of the distal phalangeal tufts, requiring evaluation of specific patterns like tuft, band-like, or periarticular resorption [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: pencil-in-cup deformity with marginal erosions and periostitis [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: preserved bone mineralization with associated nail changes [3, 5]
supportingFeatures: Sausage digit soft-tissue swelling [3, 6]
Bony ankylosis of distal interphalangeal joints [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: distal tuft resorption and overlying soft-tissue loss [7, 8]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: bilateral frostbite findings classically spare the thumbs [7, 8]
supportingFeatures: History of thermal or electrical burns [9]
Premature growth plate fusion in children [7]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: licked candy cane tapering of the distal phalanges [10, 11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concentric bone atrophy often with vascular calcifications [11, 12]
supportingFeatures: Painless presentation leading to neuroarthropathic joints [12]
Clawlike hand deformity in leprosy [13]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: tuft resorption with severe soft-tissue atrophy [14, 15]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated subcutaneous and periarticular calcinosis circumscripta [15]
supportingFeatures: Flexion contractures of the fingers [4]
Associated lower lobe pulmonary fibrosis [14]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: tuft resorption with radial middle phalangeal resorption [16, 17]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subperiosteal resorption on radial middle phalanges [16, 17]
supportingFeatures: Presence of brown tumors and osteosclerosis [16, 17]
Can also present as band-like midshaft resorption [18]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: band-like terminal tuft lucencies with generalized osteolysis [19]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated with wormian bones and dysmorphic facies [19]
supportingFeatures: Rare autosomal dominant familial inheritance [19]
Associated with basilar invagination [19]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: transverse radiolucent band across the distal phalanx [19, 20]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: midshaft resorption sparing the distal tuft tip [18]
supportingFeatures: Associated with occupational polyvinyl chloride (PVC) exposure [9, 21]
May also be seen in primary hyperparathyroidism [18, 20]

ch25.clinicalPearl: Hyperparathyroidism is unique as the only common condition that causes band-like resorption of the midportion of the phalanx [18].

ch26.patternSummary: A bowed tibia requires careful assessment of the direction of bowing, bone density, and metaphyseal integrity to distinguish between congenital dysplasias, metabolic conditions, and benign physiologic variants [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: anterior bowing with cortical thickening and coarsened trabeculae [1, 3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: anterior bowing with coarsened trabeculae in older adults [1, 4]
supportingFeatures: Associated with transverse 'banana' insufficiency fractures [3, 4].
Classic 'blade of grass' lytic leading edge may be present [5, 6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: metaphyseal fraying, splaying, and cupping with widened physes [1, 4, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: widened physes with cupped and frayed metaphyses [4, 7, 8]
supportingFeatures: Generalized osteopenia and bowing of softened diaphyses [4, 9].
May show 'rachitic rosary' at the costochondral junctions [8, 10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: symmetric gentle bowing without focal destructive bone lesions [11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: normal bone mineralization resolving spontaneously with growth [1, 2]
supportingFeatures: Typically bilateral and resolves spontaneously with growth [1].
Lacks periostitis, focal fractures, or underlying metabolic disease [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse osteopenia with multiple fractures and slender bones [1, 4, 12]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: severe osteopenia with multiple fractures of differing ages [12-14]
supportingFeatures: Healing fractures frequently show exuberant callus formation [12, 13].
Often accompanied by multiple wormian bones and platyspondyly [12, 13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: anterolateral bowing at the middle and distal thirds [1, 11, 15]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: anterolateral bowing frequently complicated by focal pseudoarthrosis [1, 11, 16]
supportingFeatures: Often associated with hypoplasia of the adjacent fibula [11, 15].
Pseudoarthroses typically display 'pencil-pointing' of the bone ends [15, 16].

ch26.clinicalPearl: The direction of bowing is highly specific: neurofibromatosis characteristically causes anterolateral bowing, whereas Paget disease classically causes anterior bowing [1, 2].

ch27.patternSummary: Diffuse periosteal reaction in adults requires careful evaluation of symmetry, specific bone involvement, and the presence of underlying systemic or malignant diseases to narrow the differential [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: smooth symmetric diaphyseal and metaphyseal periosteal reaction [1, 3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strictly spares the epiphyses, unlike primary pachydermoperiostosis [1, 3]
supportingFeatures: Associated with clubbing of the digits [1]
Frequently secondary to bronchogenic carcinoma [4]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: spiculated feathery periostitis in hands and feet [3, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: asymmetrical distribution favoring radial aspect of digits [5]
supportingFeatures: Occurs following treatment for hyperthyroidism [5]
Associated with prominent soft tissue swelling and clubbing [3]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: thick undulating periosteal reaction of lower limbs [6, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strictly involves lower limbs with overlying skin changes [6]
supportingFeatures: Lacks aggressive features or underlying bone destruction [6]
Often associated with soft tissue defects, phleboliths, or ulcer bandages [6, 7]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: aggressive periosteal reaction with permeative bone destruction [8, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated with diffuse marrow replacement and lytic lesions [8]
supportingFeatures: Transverse lucent metaphyseal bands may be present [10]
Often associated with diffuse osteopenia [11]

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse osteosclerosis with subperiosteal bone resorption [12, 13]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: subperiosteal resorption along radial aspects of middle phalanges [12, 13]
supportingFeatures: Presence of a 'rugger-jersey' spine [14]
Associated with vascular and periarticular soft-tissue calcifications [12]

ch27.clinicalPearl: Always request a chest radiograph when encountering a new, smooth, symmetric periosteal reaction in an adult, as this is the classic manifestation of hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy, most commonly secondary to lung carcinoma [4, 15].

ch28.patternSummary: Osteonecrosis (avascular necrosis) of the hip reflects ischemic bone death, classically demonstrating a subchondral crescent sign on radiographs and a pathognomonic double-line sign on MRI [1-4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: serpiginous double-line sign on T2-weighted MRI [3, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: renal transplant clips or H-shaped vertebrae present [6, 7]
supportingFeatures: Subchondral crescent sign indicating imminent articular collapse [1, 3].
Bilateral femoral head involvement is highly common [1, 8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: subchondral lucency with patchy femoral head sclerosis [4, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent pancreatic calcifications from chronic pancreatitis [10, 11]
supportingFeatures: Progresses to articular surface collapse and fragmentation [4].
Often bilateral and symmetric in presentation [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple serpiginous medullary bone infarcts [12]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: vascular calcifications or history of Caisson disease [13, 14]
supportingFeatures: Diaphyseal and metaphyseal infarcts are characteristic [12, 15].
Central fat signal surrounded by a sclerotic rim [16].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: marrow expansion with Erlenmeyer flask deformity [17, 18]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: massive splenomegaly with widespread osteopenia [10, 17]
supportingFeatures: Decreased marrow signal on T1 and T2 sequences [19].
Associated with multiple bone infarcts and pathological fractures [20, 21].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral epiphyseal fragmentation and subchondral fracture [22, 23]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: idiopathic presentation in male child aged 4-8 [11, 22]
supportingFeatures: Small, sclerotic femoral head with widened joint space [24, 25].
Joint effusion is often present early [24, 26].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilateral subchondral collapse and sclerosis [4, 27]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: evidence of prior femoral neck fracture [28, 29]
supportingFeatures: Results from direct disruption of the femoral head blood supply [29, 30].
Surgical hardware may be present on imaging.

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: mixed lytic-sclerotic process with diffuse marrow changes [31, 32]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: systemic connective tissue or metabolic storage disease signs [4, 14]
supportingFeatures: Diffuse replacement of normal fatty marrow [33].
MRI is the most efficacious way to evaluate extent [34].

ch28.clinicalPearl: When evaluating avascular necrosis of the femoral heads, always check the abdomen on the radiograph: a calcified spleen points to sickle cell disease, massive splenomegaly suggests Gaucher disease, and pancreatic calcifications indicate alcohol abuse [10, 35, 36].

ch31.patternSummary: Terminal ileal mass or stricture requires differentiating between inflammatory conditions and neoplasms based on length of involvement, symmetry, and extramural findings like lymphadenopathy or desmoplasia [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: string sign with transmural bowel wall thickening [1, 3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: discontinuous skip lesions and creeping mesenteric fat [4-6]
supportingFeatures: Aphthous ulcers progressing to 'rose-thorn' and 'cobblestone' ulceration [7, 8].
Asymmetrical mucosal involvement, commonly at the mesenteric border [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: thick-walled infiltrating mass with aneurysmal dilatation [9, 10]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: bulky lymphadenopathy without significant bowel obstruction [11, 12]
supportingFeatures: Often involves a single long loop of bowel (5-20 cm) [12].
Extensive retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly strongly support this diagnosis [12].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: hypervascular mass with spiculated mesenteric desmoplastic reaction [10, 13]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: spiculated mesenteric mass with calcification [10, 14]
supportingFeatures: Distal or terminal ileum is the most common primary site [13, 15].
Frequently associated with hypervascular liver metastases [14, 15].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: conical shrunken cecum with narrowed terminal ileum [3, 16]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: necrotic pericaecal lymph nodes with low attenuation centers [17, 18]
supportingFeatures: Stierlin sign: rapid emptying of the narrowed terminal ileum [3, 19].
Fleischner sign: wide gaping patulous ileocaecal valve [3, 19].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: short, well-demarcated circumferential narrowing or apple-core lesion [20, 21]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: focal, irregular stricture less than five centimeters long [12]
supportingFeatures: Typically produces a narrow, rigid stricture with prestenotic dilatation [21].
Rare in the ileum; more commonly found in the duodenum or jejunum [22].

ch31.clinicalPearl: Tuberculosis typically has more pronounced cecal involvement with necrotic lymph nodes, whereas Crohn's disease favors the terminal ileum and classically presents with skip lesions and creeping fat [1, 2, 18].

ch33.patternSummary: Evaluating a solid mesenteric or peritoneal mass requires careful assessment of calcification, vessel encasement versus displacement, and associated desmoplastic reactions to narrow the differential [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: misty mesentery with preserved fat around vessels
differentials.keyDiscriminator: fat ring or fat halo sign around vessels
supportingFeatures: Pseudocapsule sign with a thin fibrotic rim [3].
Encasement of mesenteric vessels without occlusion [4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: spiculated mesenteric mass with focal calcifications
differentials.keyDiscriminator: stellate desmoplastic reaction causing tethering of bowel loops
supportingFeatures: Associated intensely hypervascular liver metastases [5, 6].
Primary enhancing mass often located in the distal ileum [6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: omental caking with nodular peritoneal thickening
differentials.keyDiscriminator: malignant ascites with discrete nodular peritoneal implants
supportingFeatures: Often results from ovarian, gastrointestinal, or breast primaries [7, 8].
Frequently causes scalloping of the visceral organ surfaces [9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bulky confluent nodal masses encasing mesenteric vessels
differentials.keyDiscriminator: sandwich sign without obstruction of adjacent vessels
supportingFeatures: Often associated with splenomegaly and extensive retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy [6, 10].
Demonstrates homogeneous contrast enhancement [11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: discrete solid soft-tissue mass displacing bowel loops
differentials.keyDiscriminator: displaces rather than encases adjacent mesenteric vessels
supportingFeatures: Frequently associated with prior surgical scars or Gardner syndrome [12, 13].
Demonstrates low signal intensity on T1 and T2 MRI [13, 14].

ch33.clinicalPearl: Lymphoma characteristically encases mesenteric vessels without obstructing them (the 'sandwich sign'), whereas carcinoid tumors produce an intense desmoplastic reaction that tethers and kinks adjacent bowel loops [6, 11, 15].

ch32.patternSummary: Colitis presents with bowel wall thickening and mucosal changes, requiring assessment of distribution, symmetry, and clinical history to differentiate infectious, inflammatory, and ischemic causes [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: cobblestone mucosa or accordion sign with wall thickening
differentials.keyDiscriminator: transmural skip lesions or massive diffuse wall thickening
supportingFeatures: Crohn's classically involves the terminal ileum with fistulae and skip lesions [4].
C. difficile presents with the accordion sign and severe wall thickening [5, 6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: continuous mucosal involvement with lead pipe colon morphology
differentials.keyDiscriminator: continuous proximal extension from rectum without skip lesions
supportingFeatures: Chronic cases develop a shortened, ahaustral, featureless lead pipe colon [7].
Associated with toxic megacolon and backwash ileitis [8, 9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: aphthoid or deep ulcers mimicking Crohn's disease
differentials.keyDiscriminator: ileocecal ulcers resembling Crohn's with classic clinical triad
supportingFeatures: Radiologically mimics Crohn's disease with aphthoid ulceration [10].
Can occasionally simulate idiopathic ulcerative proctocolitis [10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: segmental thumbprinting classically located at the splenic flexure
differentials.keyDiscriminator: distribution strictly localized to vascular watershed territories
supportingFeatures: Thumbprinting represents mucosal edema or submucosal hemorrhage [11].
May progress to pneumatosis intestinalis or portal venous gas in severe necrosis [3, 12].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: extensive bowel wall thickening with large deep ulcers
differentials.keyDiscriminator: severe deep ulcerating colitis in an immunocompromised patient
supportingFeatures: Most common gastrointestinal infection in immunocompromised patients [13].
Superficial progressing to deep ulceration that can closely mimic Crohn's disease [13].

ch32.clinicalPearl: Ischemic colitis classically targets the splenic flexure watershed area, whereas ulcerative colitis almost always begins in the rectum and involves contiguous segments without skip lesions [9, 14].

ch29.patternSummary: Enhancing liver lesion — key imaging pattern for FRCR 2B (Gastrointestinal)

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Discontinuous nodular peripheral enhancement
OCP use + Fat/Haemorrhage
Central scar + Arterial enhancement
Wash-in/Wash-out + Pseudo-capsule

ch29.clinicalPearl: Key exam pattern: HAFM for Enhancing liver lesion

ch30.patternSummary: Pneumobilia — key imaging pattern for FRCR 2B (Gastrointestinal)

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Free air + Rigid abdomen
Permanent air + surgical clips
Rigler Triad: SBO + Air + Stone
Air in GB wall + Diabetes
Air in central ducts post-procedure

ch30.clinicalPearl: Key exam pattern: PAGES for Pneumobilia

ch34.patternSummary: Renal papillary necrosis results from ischemic necrobiosis of the medullary pyramids, classically demonstrating a 'ball on tee', 'lobster claw', or 'signet ring' sign on excretory urography [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: focal wedge-shaped low attenuation or striated nephrogram [4, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: unilateral asymmetric perinephric stranding in an infected patient [4]
supportingFeatures: Usually unilateral, contrasting with systemic bilateral causes [6, 7].
Patients typically present with fever, dysuria, and leukocytosis [8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: calyceal blunting with proximal collecting system dilatation [9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: identifiable obstructing calculus or mass causing hydronephrosis [10]
supportingFeatures: Usually unilateral depending on the level of obstruction [11].
Increased intraluminal pressure compromises medullary perfusion [11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: calyceal excavations with H-shaped or fish-mouth vertebrae [12]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent H-shaped vertebrae or small calcified spleen [1, 13]
supportingFeatures: Hypertonic hypoxic medullary environment exacerbates red blood cell sickling [1, 11].
May have associated avascular necrosis of the femoral heads [14, 15].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: moth-eaten calyces progressing to a calcified putty kidney [16]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: amorphous parenchymal calcifications with beaded ureteral strictures [17]
supportingFeatures: Classically presents with sterile pyuria [18, 19].
Infection causes vasculitis and subsequent vascular insufficiency leading to necrosis [6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: papillary sloughing with nodular cirrhotic liver contour [20]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: stigmata of portal hypertension and chronic liver disease [20]
supportingFeatures: Ethanol abuse contributes to dehydration and ischemic medullary damage [2, 21].
Look for associated ascites, splenomegaly, or varices [20].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: ring-shaped papillary calcifications in bilaterally small kidneys [22]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: bilateral ring calcifications with irregular scarred renal margins [23]
supportingFeatures: Most common in middle-aged women with excessive NSAID intake [6].
Exam films may simultaneously show an ankylosed 'bamboo spine' indicating NSAID use for pain [24].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: unilaterally enlarged kidney with delayed dense nephrogram [25, 26]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: enlarged kidney with delayed excretion or venous filling defect [25]
supportingFeatures: Usually unilateral and secondary to a hypercoagulable state or nephrotic syndrome [26].
Venous obstruction leads to profound renal edema and resultant ischemia [26].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lobster-claw or ball-on-tee calyceal contrast excavations [3, 27]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: frequently bilateral with concurrent vascular wall calcifications [3]
supportingFeatures: The single most common cause of renal papillary necrosis (50% of cases) [21].
Results from profound small vessel ischemic disease [3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple wedge-shaped infarcts with papillary tissue loss [28]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent multiple microaneurysms or distal arterial stenoses [29]
supportingFeatures: Includes polyarteritis nodosa, systemic lupus erythematosus, or drug abuse [30].
Systemic multi-organ involvement with patchy, asymmetric distribution [28].

ch34.clinicalPearl: When an IVU demonstrates renal papillary necrosis, always scrutinize the bones: H-shaped vertebrae indicate sickle cell disease, whereas a bamboo spine suggests analgesic nephropathy. [1, 24]

ch35.patternSummary: Nephrocalcinosis — key imaging pattern for FRCR 2B (Genitourinary)

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Bouquet of flowers + Ectatic ducts
Dense medullary calc + Alkaline urine
CORTICAL + Medullary calc
Hypercalciuria + Medullary calc
Subperiosteal resorption + Salt/Pepper skull

ch35.clinicalPearl: Key exam pattern: MARSH for Nephrocalcinosis

ch36.patternSummary: Ring-enhancing brain lesions — key imaging pattern for FRCR 2B (Neuroradiology)

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: Thin ring + Grey-white junction
Thin ring + DWI restricted center
Thick shaggy wall + midline cross
Gyral enhancement + vascular territory
Surface blood + Trauma
Open ring sign (facing grey)
Swiss-cheese pattern + field respect

ch36.clinicalPearl: Key exam pattern: MAGIC DR for Ring-enhancing brain lesions

ch37.patternSummary: Cerebellopontine angle masses are primarily extra-axial tumors and cysts, best differentiated by their enhancement patterns, extension into the internal auditory canal, and diffusion-weighted imaging characteristics [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: enhancing mass expanding the internal auditory canal [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: acute angle with petrous bone and IAC widening [2, 5]
supportingFeatures: Bilateral masses are pathognomonic for Neurofibromatosis type 2 [3, 6].
Large tumors frequently exhibit central cystic areas and hemorrhage [3, 4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: follows CSF signal on all sequences without enhancement [5, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: suppresses on FLAIR and lacks restricted diffusion [8, 9]
supportingFeatures: Exerts mass effect by displacing rather than engulfing adjacent cranial nerves and vessels [10, 11].
