Aneurysms, a rare differential, will show prominent flow voids or pulsation artifacts [12, 13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: broad-based avidly enhancing mass with dural tail [5, 14]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: obtuse angle with petrous bone and adjacent hyperostosis [2, 5]
supportingFeatures: Frequently calcifies and may cause hyperostosis of the adjacent skull base [15, 16].
Typically does not expand the internal auditory canal [14, 17].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lobulated cystic mass with restricted diffusion on DWI [8, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: hyperintense on DWI and FLAIR compared to CSF [8, 9]
supportingFeatures: Tends to engulf rather than displace adjacent cranial nerves and vessels [10, 11].
Has a 'dirty CSF' appearance, being slightly hyperintense to CSF on T1-weighted images [9, 11].

ch37.clinicalPearl: Restricted diffusion on DWI reliably distinguishes an epidermoid cyst from an arachnoid cyst, while widening of the internal auditory canal helps differentiate a vestibular schwannoma from a meningioma [2, 9, 18].

ch38.patternSummary: Pituitary region masses are best differentiated by pinpointing their anatomical epicenter (sellar vs. suprasellar), evaluating for calcifications or cysts, and assessing contrast enhancement patterns [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multilobulated cystic and solid mass with calcifications
differentials.keyDiscriminator: suprasellar epicenter with prominent calcifications and cysts
supportingFeatures: Peak incidence in childhood and the fifth decade [2, 3].
Solid components demonstrate vivid contrast enhancement [2, 4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined non-enhancing cystic sellar lesion
differentials.keyDiscriminator: entirely lacks contrast enhancement and solid calcification
supportingFeatures: Typically located centrally between the anterior and posterior pituitary lobes [5, 6].
Variable T1 signal depending on proteinaceous fluid content [7, 8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: snowman-shaped sellar mass or vascular flow void
differentials.keyDiscriminator: expands sella or demonstrates internal vascular flow voids
supportingFeatures: Macroadenomas (>10mm) homogeneously enhance and expand the pituitary fossa [2, 8].
Aneurysms may show a partially thrombosed laminated appearance [9, 10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: broad-based enhancing mass with a dural tail
differentials.keyDiscriminator: distinct from pituitary gland with adjacent hyperostosis
supportingFeatures: Meningiomas frequently calcify and cause adjacent skull base hyperostosis [11, 12].
Metastases enhance rapidly and often involve the infundibulum [13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: solid intensely enhancing mass along the neurohypophysis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: solid tumor centered strictly on the posterior pituitary
supportingFeatures: Rare benign low-grade glioma presenting in adults [14].
Presents with visual field deficits or hypopituitarism [14].

ch38.clinicalPearl: An expansile mass centered within the sella is most likely a pituitary macroadenoma, whereas a mass with a suprasellar epicenter containing cysts and calcifications is classic for a craniopharyngioma [15].

ch39.patternSummary: Symmetrical bilateral basal ganglia abnormalities require distinguishing between benign age-related calcifications, inherited metabolic disorders, and acquired toxic or hypoxic injuries [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: extensive symmetric basal ganglia and dentate nucleus calcification [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent extensive calcification of the dentate nuclei [3, 4]
supportingFeatures: Familial neurodegenerative disease presenting with movement disorders [3, 4].
Strictly normal calcium and parathyroid hormone levels [4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: faint bilateral punctate globus pallidus calcifications [1]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strictly limited to the globus pallidus without mass effect [1, 5]
supportingFeatures: The most common cause of basal ganglia calcification [6].
Incidental asymptomatic finding that increases with age [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: extensive symmetric basal ganglia and subcortical calcification [7, 8]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent calvarial thickening or short fourth metacarpals [7, 9, 10]
supportingFeatures: Associated with hypocalcemia and low or resistant parathyroid hormone [11, 12].
Often accompanied by soft tissue or ligamentous calcifications [9, 10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: symmetric low-attenuation necrosis in the globus pallidus [13, 14]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: striking predilection for the globus pallidus bilaterally [13, 14]
supportingFeatures: Acute phase may show restricted diffusion on MRI [14].
May chronically develop delayed leukoencephalopathy in deep white matter [15].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral low density in putamina and thalami [16, 17]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent loss of cerebral gray-white matter differentiation [17, 18]
supportingFeatures: May show the reversal sign with a relatively dense cerebellum [17, 19].
Restricted diffusion in deep gray matter and watershed zones [2, 20].

ch39.clinicalPearl: When encountering extensive, symmetrical basal ganglia calcification, always check serum calcium to differentiate hypoparathyroidism from Fahr disease, and look at a hand radiograph for short fourth metacarpals indicating pseudohypoparathyroidism [4, 10].

ch40.patternSummary: Evaluating an intra-axial haemorrhage requires careful assessment of lesion location, multiplicity, and surrounding oedema to narrow the differential diagnosis [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: haemorrhage at the grey-white matter junction
differentials.keyDiscriminator: disproportionately large amount of surrounding vasogenic oedema
supportingFeatures: Commonly from melanoma, renal, choriocarcinoma, or thyroid primaries [2].
Often presents as multiple lesions [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: spontaneous lobar subcortical haemorrhage with microbleeds
differentials.keyDiscriminator: gradient echo microbleeds strictly sparing deep grey matter
supportingFeatures: Typically presents in older age groups with concurrent cerebral atrophy [3, 4].
AVMs present with a tangle of flow voids on MRI [5].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: superficial parenchymal haemorrhage adjacent to bone
differentials.keyDiscriminator: predilection for inferior frontal and anterior temporal poles
supportingFeatures: Classically located where the brain abuts the bony calvarium [6].
Often associated with skull fractures and extra-axial haemorrhages [3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: large parenchymal hematoma with fluid-blood levels
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated with bleeding diathesis or anticoagulant use
supportingFeatures: Results from anticoagulants or bleeding diatheses like hemophilia [7, 8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: acute hematoma centered in the basal ganglia
differentials.keyDiscriminator: strict predilection for deep grey matter and brainstem
supportingFeatures: Most common locations are the putamen, thalamus, pons, and cerebellum [9].

ch40.clinicalPearl: The anatomical location is a crucial differentiator: hypertensive bleeds strictly favor the deep grey matter, whereas amyloid angiopathy and metastases characteristically cause lobar or subcortical haemorrhages [3, 4, 10].

ch41.patternSummary: Restricted diffusion on MRI indicates constrained water mobility, critical for differentiating acute ischemia, highly cellular tumors, and purulent infections [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: central restricted diffusion with peripheral rim enhancement
differentials.keyDiscriminator: restricted diffusion centrally with a T2-hypointense capsule
supportingFeatures: Significant surrounding vasogenic edema [4].
Ring enhancement is typically thinner on the medial margin [5].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: solid enhancing mass with restricted diffusion
differentials.keyDiscriminator: iso- to hypointense on T2WI due to hypercellularity
supportingFeatures: Characteristically involves the deep gray matter and corpus callosum [6].
Relatively little mass effect or edema for its size [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: cortical restricted diffusion conforming to vascular territory
differentials.keyDiscriminator: wedge-shaped cortical involvement respecting arterial boundaries
supportingFeatures: Associated with loss of grey-white matter differentiation [8].
ADC pseudonormalizes after 5-10 days [9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: restricted diffusion in basal ganglia and cortical ribbon
differentials.keyDiscriminator: pulvinar or hockey-stick sign in the thalamus
supportingFeatures: Strictly lacks contrast enhancement and white matter involvement [10].
Rapidly progressive dementia with generalized myoclonus [11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral asymmetric medial temporal lobe restricted diffusion
differentials.keyDiscriminator: limbic system involvement that strictly spares the putamen
supportingFeatures: Often complicated by petechial or lobar hemorrhage [12].
Can present early with normal CT findings [13, 14].

ch41.clinicalPearl: Diffusion-weighted imaging is the most reliable sequence to differentiate a ring-enhancing cerebral abscess from a necrotic glioblastoma, as only the abscess demonstrates restricted water mobility centrally [3, 15].

ch42.patternSummary: Intramedullary spinal masses lie within the substance of the spinal cord, classically causing cord expansion, and are best differentiated by patient age, lesion eccentricity, and specific MRI signal characteristics like hemosiderin or fat [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: owl's eye T2 hyperintensity within central gray matter [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: acute presentation with restricted diffusion on MRI [4]
supportingFeatures: Mild cord expansion in the acute phase, but less than tumors [5, 6].
Involves the anterior spinal artery territory centrally [5, 7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: long segment diffuse T2 hyperintensity with variable enhancement [8]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: involves entire cord cross-section without a discrete focal mass [8]
supportingFeatures: Presents with acute onset sensorimotor neurologic deficits and pain [8].
Can present as a discrete intramedullary abscess in severe infections [9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: cystic intramedullary mass with intensely enhancing mural nodule [10, 11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: associated serpentine vascular flow voids and extensive syrinx [10, 12]
supportingFeatures: Most often dorsal or subpial in location [13].
Strongly associated with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome and multiple lesions [14, 15].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: central well-defined enhancing mass with symmetric cord expansion [6, 16]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: hypointense hemosiderin cap sign at the tumor poles [16, 17]
supportingFeatures: Frequently causes bony remodeling and widening of the spinal canal [6, 18].
Intratumoral cysts and hemorrhage are very common [16, 19].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: eccentric ill-defined infiltrative mass causing fusiform cord expansion [10, 20]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: eccentric location with poorly defined margins and patchy enhancement [2, 10]
supportingFeatures: Most common intramedullary spinal tumor in children [1, 20].
Unlike ependymomas, does not typically cause adjacent bony remodeling [18].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined homogeneous mass with high T1 signal intensity [21]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: pure fat signal intensity completely suppressing on STIR [21]
supportingFeatures: Commonly located at the conus medullaris or filum terminale [21].
Often associated with spinal dysraphism or tethered cord syndrome [22, 23].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: solid homogeneously enhancing mass with extensive cord edema [24]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: iso- to hypointense on T2 due to hypercellularity [25, 26]
supportingFeatures: Disproportionately large area of cord edema relative to the lesion size [24, 27].
May present with concurrent leptomeningeal 'sugar coating' [25, 28].

ch42.clinicalPearl: Patient age is a crucial differentiator: astrocytomas are the most common intramedullary tumor in children, whereas ependymomas are the most common in adults [1, 2].

ch43.patternSummary: Intradural extramedullary masses lie within the cerebrospinal fluid space but outside the spinal cord, characteristically being surrounded by CSF and displacing the cord without expanding it [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: broad-based enhancing mass or nodular leptomeningeal coating [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: presence of dural tail or widespread CSF drop metastases [3, 4]
supportingFeatures: Meningiomas heavily favor the thoracic spine in middle-aged females [3, 5].
Metastases often present as multiple enhancing nodules ('sugar coating') [4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: enhancing mass at the conus or filum terminale [6]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: arises specifically from the conus medullaris or filum terminale [6]
supportingFeatures: The most common neoplasm of the conus medullaris [6].
Demonstrates almost constant contrast enhancement [6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: dumbbell-shaped mass expanding the intervertebral neural foramen [3, 4]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: extends through and distinctly widens the intervertebral exit foramen [7]
supportingFeatures: May exhibit a T2 'target' sign with a low signal center [3].
Multiple lesions strongly suggest underlying neurofibromatosis [7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: fat-containing cystic mass with heterogeneous T1 signal [8, 9]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: macroscopic fat signal with an associated dorsal dermal sinus [9, 10]
supportingFeatures: Frequently associated with spinal dysraphism and a dorsal dermal sinus tract [10].
Rupture causes dissemination of hyperintense lipid droplets throughout the CSF [9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: intrathecal nodular enhancing masses coating the spinal cord [11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: nodular pial enhancement with concurrent systemic or pulmonary disease [12]
supportingFeatures: Presents as contrast-enhancing nodules coating the spinal cord and pia-arachnoid [11, 13].
Diagnosis is often inferred from known systemic noncaseating granulomatous disease [12].

ch43.clinicalPearl: A mass that assumes a dumbbell shape and widens the neural foramen is characteristic of a nerve sheath tumor, whereas a broad-based mass with a dural tail in the thoracic spine strongly favors a meningioma [3, 7].

ch44.patternSummary: Extradural spinal masses arise outside the thecal sac, often from adjacent vertebrae, intervertebral discs, or epidural fat, and characteristically displace the thecal sac inward [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: marrow infiltration contiguous with extradural soft tissue mass [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: destruction of the vertebral pedicle or posterior elements [4, 5]
supportingFeatures: Metastases typically involve the posterior elements and pedicles, unlike myeloma which may initially spare them [5, 6].
Commonly presents as multiple lesions with pathologic compression fractures [5, 7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: long segment extradural collection with peripheral rim enhancement [1, 8]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent discitis or vertebral osteomyelitis [9, 10]
supportingFeatures: Considered a neurosurgical emergency due to the severe risk of spinal cord compression [11].
Demonstrates restricted diffusion and often tracks dorsolaterally spanning multiple vertebral levels [12, 13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: focal extradural mass contiguous with the intervertebral disc [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: follows the signal intensity of the parent disc [14]
supportingFeatures: Often accompanied by signs of disc desiccation and height loss [14, 15].
Sequestered or free fragments may migrate superiorly or inferiorly from the parent disc [16, 17].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: corduroy vertebral body with epidural soft tissue extension [18, 19]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: high T1 and T2 signal with thickened trabeculae [18, 19]
supportingFeatures: Displays a characteristic 'polka-dot' appearance on axial CT imaging [18].
The larger the degree of fat overgrowth, the less likely the lesion will be symptomatic [20].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: homogeneous soft tissue mass or excessive epidural fat [3, 21, 22]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: intense homogeneous enhancement or pure unenhancing fat signal [21, 22]
supportingFeatures: Lymphoma is typically hyperdense on CT, isointense on T1, and enhances intensely and homogeneously [21].
Lipomatosis follows pure fat signal on all sequences, does not enhance, and is usually secondary to exogenous steroid use [22].

ch44.clinicalPearl: A spinal epidural abscess is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent action to prevent irreversible paraplegia, which distinguishes it critically from benign causes of extradural masses like disc disease or lipomatosis [9, 11].

ch45.patternSummary: Evaluating a diffuse periosteal reaction in a child requires assessing symmetry, patient age, and the presence of metaphyseal involvement to differentiate physiological processes from aggressive pathologies [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: dense provisional calcification or bilateral luetic diaphysitis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: epiphyseal sclerotic ring or bilateral medial tibial erosions
supportingFeatures: Scurvy classically presents with subperiosteal hemorrhage and a dense zone of provisional calcification (Frankel line) [3].
Syphilis can cause bilateral erosions of the proximal medial tibial metaphyses, known as the Wimberger sign [1, 4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: transverse lucent metaphyseal bands with diffuse osteopenia
differentials.keyDiscriminator: bilateral symmetric metaphyseal lucencies in a young child
supportingFeatures: Periosteal new bone formation occurs secondary to subperiosteal leukemic cellular infiltration [5].
Peak incidence of leukemia is between 2 and 5 years of age [5, 6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: ill-defined lytic lesion with sequestrum and periostitis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: thick irregular reactive sclerosis or crossing open physis
supportingFeatures: Often initially affects the metaphysis due to slow-flowing venous sinusoids [6, 7].
May demonstrate the penumbra sign on T1-weighted MRI sequences [8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: symmetric smooth diaphyseal periostitis strictly sparing the metaphyses
differentials.keyDiscriminator: spares metaphyses in infants or known cardiac history
supportingFeatures: Physiological periostitis occurs between 1 and 6 months of age and resolves spontaneously [2].
Prostaglandin therapy causes a similar appearance but occurs in patients treated for ductus-dependent congenital heart disease [9, 10].

ch45.clinicalPearl: Physiologic periosteal reaction strictly spares the metaphyses and only occurs between 1 and 6 months of age, whereas pathologic processes like leukemia and infection commonly involve the metaphyses [2, 11].

ch46.patternSummary: Vertebra plana refers to the uniform severe flattening of a vertebral body, and in children, it requires urgent differentiation between benign histiocytosis and aggressive neoplasms or infection [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: uniform coin-on-edge flattening of single vertebral body
differentials.keyDiscriminator: preserves adjacent intervertebral discs and spares posterior elements
supportingFeatures: The most common cause of vertebra plana in children [2, 3].
May have associated lytic skull lesions with beveled edges or a button sequestrum [4, 5].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: permeative vertebral destruction with paraspinal soft tissue mass
differentials.keyDiscriminator: aggressive permeative destruction extending into the posterior elements
supportingFeatures: Most common nonlymphoproliferative primary malignant spinal tumor in children [6].
Can present with fever and leukocytosis mimicking osteomyelitis [7, 8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined lucency with rings and arcs calcification
differentials.keyDiscriminator: chondroid matrix mineralization without periosteal reaction or pain
supportingFeatures: Exceedingly rare in the spine; most common in small tubular bones of hands and feet [9].
Lacks pain or aggressive features unless complicated by a pathologic fracture [10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multilevel permeative vertebral body collapse and sclerosis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: multilevel spine involvement in child under five years
supportingFeatures: Often arises from a primary adrenal mass and frequently metastasizes to bone [3, 11].
Frequent metastasis to the skull base causing 'raccoon eyes' clinically [12, 13].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple wedge compression fractures with diffuse osteopenia
differentials.keyDiscriminator: multiple lytic lesions characteristically sparing the vertebral pedicles
supportingFeatures: Occurs almost exclusively in older adults (>40 years), not children [14, 15].
Solitary plasmacytomas may present with a pathognomonic 'mini-brain' appearance on MRI [16, 17].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: vertebral endplate destruction with disc space narrowing
differentials.keyDiscriminator: infection centered on intervertebral disc involving adjacent vertebrae
supportingFeatures: Bacterial infection rapidly destroys the disc, whereas TB may relatively spare the disc space [18, 19].
Frequently associated with epidural, subligamentous, or paraspinal abscesses [20, 21].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: calcified paravertebral mass invading the spinal canal
differentials.keyDiscriminator: mass extending through neural foramina encasing adjacent vessels
supportingFeatures: The most common abdominal malignancy in infancy [22].
Unlike Wilms tumor, it frequently encases rather than displaces adjacent major vessels [23, 24].

ch46.clinicalPearl: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma) is by far the most common cause of vertebra plana in children, classically presenting with uniform vertebral collapse and completely intact adjacent intervertebral disc spaces [1-3].

ch47.patternSummary: Multiple wormian bones, defined as more than ten intrasutural ossicles in the cranial vault, signify an underlying skeletal dysplasia or metabolic disorder [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: generalized osteosclerosis with pointed terminal phalangeal tufts [4, 5]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: increased bone density with an obtuse mandibular angle [5]
supportingFeatures: Often presents with short stature and multiple fractures [5].
Preserved medullary canal distinguishes it from osteopetrosis [5].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse osteopenia with multiple fractures and bowing [6, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: severe osteopenia with fractures of differing ages [6, 7]
supportingFeatures: Fractures frequently show exuberant callus formation [6, 7].
Associated with blue sclerae and hearing impairment [8, 9].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: metaphyseal cupping, splaying, and fraying with bowing [10, 11]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: widened physes with generalized osteopenia and bone softening [12, 13]
supportingFeatures: May show a 'rachitic rosary' at the costochondral junctions [10].
Wormian bones are characteristically seen during the healing phase [3, 14].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: metaphyseal irregularity mimicking non-accidental trauma [15]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: metaphyseal fractures in a patient with kinky hair [15]
supportingFeatures: Neurodegenerative disorder due to a defect in copper metabolism [16].
Typically lethal within the first year of life [16].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: bilateral absent or hypoplastic clavicles [2, 17]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: defective clavicles with normal overall bone density [2]
supportingFeatures: Wide pubic symphysis and coxa vara [2].
Delayed eruption of multiple supernumerary teeth [18].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: delayed skeletal maturation with fragmented stippled epiphyses [19, 20]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: delayed skeletal maturation and bullet-shaped lumbar vertebrae [21]
supportingFeatures: Wide cranial sutures and fontanels with delayed closure [20, 21].
May show dense metaphyseal bands [21].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: flared iliac wings and hypoplastic facial bones [22]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: decreased iliac angle with atlanto-axial subluxation [22, 23]
supportingFeatures: Hypoplasia of facial bones and paranasal sinuses [22-24].
Two ossification centers in the manubrium (90%) [25].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: lamellar periosteal proliferation of long bone diaphyses [26]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: diaphyseal periostitis with clubbing in an older child [26, 27]
supportingFeatures: Primary hypertrophic osteoarthropathy presenting with skin thickening [27].
Characteristically spares the epiphyses [27].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: dense provisional calcification zone and subperiosteal hematomas [28, 29]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: sclerotic Wimberger ring around a low-density epiphysis [29]
supportingFeatures: Pelkan spurs projecting at right angles to the metaphysis [28].
Results from vitamin C deficiency causing ground-glass osteopenia [11, 29].

ch47.clinicalPearl: To quickly narrow the differential for multiple wormian bones, evaluate bone density and the clavicles: osteogenesis imperfecta presents with osteopenia, pyknodysostosis with osteosclerosis, and cleidocranial dysplasia with absent clavicles and normal bone density [2].

ch48.patternSummary: Posterior fossa masses in children are primarily primary malignancies rather than metastases, and are best differentiated by their location (midline vs. lateral), density, and enhancement patterns [1].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: diffuse enlargement of the pons
differentials.keyDiscriminator: centred in the pons without significant enhancement
supportingFeatures: Often anterior to the fourth ventricle, flattening its floor [2, 3].
Presents with focal neurology rather than hydrocephalus [3, 4].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: cystic cerebellar mass with enhancing mural nodule
differentials.keyDiscriminator: cyst with intensely enhancing peripheral solid nodule
supportingFeatures: Most common infratentorial tumor in children [5].
May be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1 [6, 7].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: hyperdense midline mass arising from the vermis
differentials.keyDiscriminator: hyperdense on non-contrast CT with restricted diffusion
supportingFeatures: Arises from the roof of the fourth ventricle [8, 9].
Prone to CSF seeding or 'drop metastases' [10, 11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: heterogeneous mass expanding the fourth ventricle
differentials.keyDiscriminator: extends through CSF foramina with frequent calcification
supportingFeatures: Plastic growth through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie [12, 13].
Contains calcification in approximately 50% of cases [12, 13].

ch48.clinicalPearl: A midline homogeneous and hyperdense mass indicates a medulloblastoma, a heterogeneous lesion expanding the fourth ventricle through its foramina indicates an ependymoma, and a cystic lesion with a small enhancing nodule indicates a juvenile pilocytic astrocytoma [1, 11].

ch49.patternSummary: Large abdominal masses in children demand rapid differentiation between congenital cystic diseases and malignant neoplasms, primarily by assessing the organ of origin and relationship to adjacent major vessels [1, 2].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: well-defined solid renal mass with claw sign [3]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: displaces rather than encases adjacent major vessels [4, 5]
supportingFeatures: Peak incidence is around three years of age [3].
May invade the renal vein and inferior vena cava [3, 6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: heterogeneous hepatic mass or communicating renal cysts [2, 7]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: hepatic origin or cysts communicating at the renal pelvis [2, 7]
supportingFeatures: Hepatoblastoma is the most common primary hepatic malignancy in infancy [7].
Hydronephrosis is the most common pediatric abdominal mass overall [8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: calcified suprarenal mass extending across the midline [9, 10]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: encases rather than displaces adjacent major abdominal vessels [4, 5]
supportingFeatures: Almost all neuroblastomas contain calcification, unlike Wilms tumors [5].
Frequently metastasizes to bone and the skull base [5, 11].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: solid neonatal renal mass or non-communicating cysts [12, 13]
differentials.keyDiscriminator: presentation in the neonatal period under 3 months [12, 14]
supportingFeatures: Mesoblastic nephroma is the most common solid renal mass in neonates [12].
Multicystic dysplastic kidney (MCDK) presents with multiple cysts that do not communicate [13].

ch49.clinicalPearl: When differentiating the two most common childhood abdominal malignancies, remember that neuroblastoma typically encases vessels and commonly calcifies, whereas Wilms tumor typically displaces vessels and rarely calcifies [4, 5].

ch50.patternSummary: Neonatal low bowel obstruction presents with multiple dilated loops and delayed meconium passage, requiring a contrast enema to differentiate mechanical from functional causes [1-3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: cone-shaped transition zone with small caliber rectum
differentials.keyDiscriminator: abnormal rectosigmoid ratio of less than one
supportingFeatures: Aganglionic segment may have a saw-tooth appearance [4, 5].
Presents with delayed meconium passage in term neonates [6].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: multiple dilated loops and secondary microcolon
differentials.keyDiscriminator: contrast enema cannot reflux into dilated small bowel
supportingFeatures: Usually results from an in utero vascular insult [7, 8].
Often associated with polyhydramnios on antenatal ultrasound [8].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: microcolon with multiple terminal ileum filling defects
differentials.keyDiscriminator: contrast refluxes into dilated proximal small bowel
supportingFeatures: Soap-bubble appearance on plain radiograph [3, 9].
Almost universally associated with cystic fibrosis [3].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: low bowel obstruction with absent anal gas
differentials.keyDiscriminator: concurrent sacral agenesis or vertebral anomalies
supportingFeatures: Associated with VACTERL sequence and Currarino triad [10, 11].
May have fistulous communication to the urinary tract [10].

differentials.dominantImagingFeature: small caliber left colon up to splenic flexure
differentials.keyDiscriminator: normal rectal caliber distinguishing it from Hirschsprung disease
supportingFeatures: Associated with maternal diabetes and prematurity [12, 13].
Contrast enema is frequently therapeutic, prompting meconium passage [13].

ch50.clinicalPearl: A contrast enema is essential for diagnosis: meconium ileus shows a microcolon with reflux into dilated small bowel, while Hirschsprung disease shows a reversed rectosigmoid ratio with a transition zone [3, 12].
